title: Why Exact Search Doesn't Scale
concept: Linear-scan cost against millions or billions of vectors, and why approximation becomes necessary.

exercises.prompt: Before we can search for similar concepts, what must we first do with our raw text data?

options.text: Convert the text into high-dimensional embedding vectors.
Store raw text strings directly in a relational SQL database.
Translate the text into another language to reduce file size.

exercises.explain: Before finding matching vectors, text must be converted into numerical vectors (embeddings) using an embedding model.

cards.title: The Billions-Vector Problem
cards.body: Finding the most similar vector in a database of a few hundred items is instant. But what happens when your AI app grows to handle billions of user memories, documents, or products?

cards.title: What is Exact Search?
cards.body: Exact search (or a linear scan) compares your query vector to every single vector in the database to find the absolute closest matches. As your database grows to millions of vectors, this brute-force approach becomes extremely slow.

cards.title: The Messy Library Analogy
cards.body: Imagine looking for a specific book in an unorganized library by reading every single page of every book. If you have only five books, it's fast; if you have millions, it's impossible.

cards.title: The Scaling Wall
cards.body: A music app with 10,000 songs can instantly find the exact closest acoustic match. But when the catalog grows to 100 million songs, comparing a user's humming to every single song vector takes several seconds, causing lag.

cards.title: The Hardware Trap
cards.body: You might think buying faster servers solves the bottleneck. However, because exact search scales linearly (O(N)), doubling the database size always doubles the work, making hardware upgrades a temporary and expensive band-aid.

cards.title: Exact vs. Approximate Search
cards.body: Comparing the scaling behavior of searching through an entire collection versus using shortcuts.

left.text: Compares query to all 1,000,000 vectors. Slow but 100% accurate.

right.text: Compares query to a tiny subset of vectors. Ultra-fast and 99% accurate.

exercises.prompt: What is the main drawback of using exact nearest neighbor search on a database of 100 million vectors?

options.text: The search gets faster as the dataset grows.
The time required grows linearly with the number of vectors.
The database automatically deletes old vectors to stay fast.
It requires complex quantum computing power to work at all.

exercises.explain: Exact search requires calculating the distance to every single item. If the number of items (N) grows from thousands to billions, the search time grows proportionally.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To keep search speeds fast at scale, we must trade absolute accuracy for search speed.
exercises.explain: Approximate search finds 'close enough' matches in a fraction of the time, which is essential for real-time applications at scale.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to describe checking every item:
exercises.sentence: Checking every vector one by one is called a linear scan.
exercises.explain: A linear scan (brute-force search) checks every single item one by one, which fails to scale when you have millions of vectors.

exercises.prompt: Which option represents an AI developer's recommendation for handling millions of vectors?

options.text: We should write a simple Python for-loop to compare our query to all 50 million database rows one by one.
To fix the speed issue, we should compress our vectors to 1-dimension and lose all semantic meaning.
Since exact search scales poorly, we need algorithms that trade a tiny bit of accuracy for massive speed gains.

exercises.explain: Option C represents an automated AI-like agent response that suggests sacrificing absolute perfection (exact match) for speed (approximation) to make the system scalable.

exercises.prompt: At scale, why must we abandon exact distance calculations (like exact cosine similarity) for every item in our vector database?

options.text: Calculations become mathematically impossible to compute.
High-dimensional vectors automatically lose their meaning over time.
Scanning every single vector scales linearly, becoming too slow for real-time apps.
Exact search requires converting all text back into raw binary files.

exercises.explain: In vector databases with millions of high-dimensional embeddings, calculating the exact distance between your query and every single vector (linear scan) takes too much time and CPU power. Approximation is required to respond in milliseconds.
